summary:
The Kingdom of Champa (c. 192–1832 AD) was a Hinduized maritime state along the central coast of modern Vietnam that for over a millennium maintained its independence against both Chinese pressure from the north and later Vietnamese expansion from the same direction. Founded originally as a rebellion against Han Chinese rule in 192 AD, Champa developed into a sophisticated Indianized culture with remarkable Hindu temple architecture — the My Son sanctuary (a UNESCO World Heritage Site) preserves dozens of Cham tower-temples built between the 4th and 14th centuries AD in a style blending Indian and indigenous Cham elements.

Champa's economy was based on maritime trade — the Cham were among the most active seafarers in Southeast Asia, with merchant ships plying routes across the South China Sea. They were also formidable raiders, periodically sacking Vietnamese cities to the north. Cham religion evolved from early Hinduism (particularly Shiva worship) to a later blend of Hinduism, Buddhism, and eventually Islam as Cham traders converted through contact with Muslim merchants. The Vietnamese kingdom of Dai Viet steadily absorbed Cham territory through a centuries-long process of conquest and annexation, finally ending Cham political independence in the 19th century. A small Cham Muslim community survives in Vietnam and Cambodia today, maintaining ancient traditions and the Cham script — one of the few surviving ancient scripts of Southeast Asia.
